Creating the bayesdir R package 1 Introduction

Creating the bayesdir R package Sheng Jiang 2026-05-13 1 Introduction This package implements bayesdir, Bayesian directional data analysis, including model 1, model 2. The documentation and package are both created using one simple command: litr::render("index.Rmd", output_format = litr::litr_gitbook()) ## install.packages("./bayesdir/", repos = NULL, type="source") This line is purely for testing (to be deleted later!). # Complete development workflow my_load <- function(){ litr::render("~/repos/bayesdir/index.Rmd", output_format = litr::litr_gitbook(minimal_eval = TRUE)) devtools::document("~/repos/bayesdir/bayesdir") # Update documentation devtools::load_all("~/repos/bayesdir/bayesdir") } my_load() # Step 2: Check the package (optional but recommended) devtools::check("~/repos/bayesdir/bayesdir", document = FALSE) # Step 3: Install the package locally for full installation devtools::install("~/repos/bayesdir/bayesdir") # Step 4: Load the installed package (optional, if you want to test installation) library(bayesdir) setwd("~/repos/bayesdir/") ## 1. Delete the auto-generated _main.Rmd file unlink("_main.Rmd") unlink("_bookdown_files", recursive = TRUE) if(!dir.create("bayesdir")){ unlink("bayesdir", recursive = TRUE) } ## 2. Re-run the render command litr::render( "index.Rmd", output_format = litr::litr_gitbook( minimal_eval = TRUE ) ) ## 3. Document and load the package devtools::document("bayesdir") devtools::load_all("bayesdir")

2 Package setup The DESCRIPTION file is created using this code. usethis::create_package( path = ".", fields = list( Package = params$package_name, Version = "0.0.0.9000", Title = "bayesdir", Description = "Bayesian dynamic network models", `Authors@R` = person( given = "Sheng", family = "Jiang", email = "jiangsheng@cuhk.edu.cn", role = c("aut", "cre") ) ) ) usethis::use_mit_license(copyright_holder = "Sheng Jiang") The following is what will show up when someone types package?bayesdir in the console. #' bayesdir #' #' This package implements the `bayesdir` method. #' #' @docType package This package will have some dependancies: library(tidyverse) ## Warning: package 'ggplot2' was built under R version 4.4.3 ##library(ggplot2) ##usethis::use_package("tidyverse", type = "depends") ## usethis::use_package("ggplot2") usethis::use_package("ggplot2", type = "depends") usethis::use_package("MASS", type = "depends") usethis::use_package("parallel", type = "depends") usethis::use_package("mvnfast", type = "depends") usethis::use_package("stats", type = "depends") usethis::use_package("matrixsampling", type = "depends") usethis::use_package("matrixNormal", type = "depends") usethis::use_package("pgdraw", type = "depends") usethis::use_package("tmvnsim", type = "depends") usethis::use_pipe(export=TRUE)

3 Helpers functions These are some helper functions that are needed for the posterior computation These functions are not documented for now. (TODO: do this). #' Compute the function A(kappa) = I_{p/2}(kappa) / I_{p/2-1}(kappa) #' for the von Mises–Fisher distribution. #' @param kappa concentration parameter (non‑negative scalar) #' @param p space dimension (default = 2) #' @return scalar value of A(kappa) get_Ap <- function(kappa, p = 2) { if (kappa < 1e-6) return(0) besselI(kappa, p/2, expon.scaled = TRUE) / besselI(kappa, p/2 - 1, expon.scaled = TRUE) } #' Compute the score function s(eta, x) = x - E[X] for vMF. #' @param eta natural parameter vector (length p) #' @param x unit vector observation (length p) #' @param p space dimension (default = 2) #' @return score vector of length p score_fn <- function(eta, x, p = 2) { kappa <- sqrt(sum(eta^2)) if (kappa < 1e-6) return(x) expected <- get_Ap(kappa, p) * eta / kappa x - expected } #' Draw a random unit vector from the vMF(eta) distribution on the circle. Used in simulation for comparison, #' Only implemented for p = 2. #' @param eta natural parameter vector (length 2) #' @return 2‑dimensional unit vector rvMF2 <- function(eta) { k <- sqrt(sum(eta^2)) if (k < 1e-6) { angle <- runif(1, 0, 2 * pi) } else { mu_angle <- atan2(eta[2], eta[1]) angle <- as.numeric(circular::rvonmises(1, circular::circular(mu_angle), k)) } c(cos(angle), sin(angle)) } #' Compute the sum of weights sum_{t = start}^{end} 1/(t + N)^2. #' @param start starting index (t >= 1) #' @param end ending index #' @param N number of observed data points #' @return scalar value of the sum compute_weight_sum <- function(start, end, N) { sum(1 / ((start:end) + N)^2) }

5 Predictive Resampler 5.1 The main sampler The main Gibbs sampler is called run_predresamp(). # ---- Main chain runner ---- #' Run a single martingale posterior chain (no preconditioner). #' #' The chain starts from the initial natural parameter c0 and performs #' predictive resampling for `nmc` steps (or only `M` steps if hybrid = TRUE, #' followed by a Gaussian correction). Optional post‑hoc variance correction #' rescales the final sample so that its covariance agrees with the asymptotic #' posterior covariance of the preconditioned version. #' #' @param c0 initial natural parameter vector (length p) #' @param N number of observed data points #' @param nmc number of predictive resampling steps (used when hybrid = FALSE) #' @param hybrid logical; if TRUE, apply hybrid acceleration with truncation at M #' @param M truncation point for hybrid acceleration (default = 20) #' @param post_correct logical; if TRUE, perform post‑hoc variance correction #' @param p space dimension (fixed to 2) #' #' @return A list with components: #' \item{c_final}{final natural parameter vector (after possible correction)} #' \item{mu_final}{mean direction (angle in radians)} #' \item{kappa_final}{concentration parameter} #' run_single_martingale <- function(c0, N, nmc, hybrid = FALSE, M = 20, post_correct = FALSE, p = 2) { c_curr <- c0 V_mat <- matrix(0, p, p) # accumulated un‑preconditioned score outer products total_weight <- 0 # sum of squared learning rates x_chain <- matrix(0,p,nmc) c_chain <- matrix(0,p,nmc) if (!hybrid) { # ---- Full predictive resampling without truncation ---- for (t in 1:nmc) { x_new <- rvMF2(c_curr) s <- score_fn(c_curr, x_new, p) wt <- 1 / (t + N) total_weight <- total_weight + wt^2 V_mat <- V_mat + wt^2 * (s %*% t(s)) c_curr <- c_curr + wt * s x_chain[,t] <- x_new c_chain[,t] <- c_curr } Sigma_used <- V_mat } else { # ---- Hybrid acceleration: M steps + Gaussian correction ---- w_cur <- compute_weight_sum(1, M, N) for (t in 1:M) { x_new <- rvMF2(c_curr) s <- score_fn(c_curr, x_new, p) wt <- 1 / (t + N) V_mat <- V_mat + wt^2 * (s %*% t(s)) c_curr <- c_curr + wt * s x_chain[,t] <- x_new c_chain[,t] <- c_curr } w_rem <- compute_weight_sum(M + 1, M + 10000, N) r <- w_rem / w_cur Cov_rem <- r * V_mat Sigma_used <- V_mat + Cov_rem # total covariance estimate # Gaussian correction eig <- eigen(Cov_rem, symmetric = TRUE) eig$values[eig$values < 0] <- 0 sqrt_Cov_rem <- eig$vectors %*% diag(sqrt(eig$values)) %*% t(eig$vectors) epsilon <- rnorm(p) c_curr <- c_curr + sqrt_Cov_rem %*% epsilon } # ---- Post‑hoc variance correction (if requested) ---- if (post_correct) { if (!hybrid) { total_w <- compute_weight_sum(1, nmc, N) } else { total_w <- compute_weight_sum(1, M, N) + compute_weight_sum(M + 1, M + 10000, N) } delta <- c_curr - c0 # Use eigendecomposition for stable inversion of the covariance matrix eigS <- eigen(Sigma_used, symmetric = TRUE) eigS$values[eigS$values < 1e-12] <- 0 inv_Sigma <- eigS$vectors %*% diag(ifelse(eigS$values > 0, 1/eigS$values, 0)) %*% t(eigS$vectors) c_curr <- c0 + total_w * (inv_Sigma %*% delta) } # ---- Convert to polar coordinates (mu, kappa) ---- kappa_final <- sqrt(sum(c_curr^2)) if (p == 2) { mu_final <- atan2(c_curr[2], c_curr[1]) if (mu_final < 0) mu_final <- mu_final + 2 * pi } else { mu_final <- NA } list(c_final = c_curr, mu_final = mu_final, kappa_final = kappa_final, x_chain = x_chain, c_chain = c_chain) }
6 Simulation Study: Bayesian vMF models for current direction Data 6.1 Sythetic data for illustration 6.2 Bayesian Inference on a conjugate prior. 6.3 Predictive Posterior via Martingale.

6 Simulation Study: Bayesian vMF models for current direction Data This vignette demonstrates a complete simulation study using bayesdir on synthetic traffic data from a grid road network. 6.1 Sythetic data for illustration 6.2 Bayesian Inference on a conjugate prior. Used as an benchmark. See Forbes & Mardia (2015) by \(\texttt{circglmbayes}\) Here \(\mu \in [0,2\pi]\) as an angle. \(\kappa \geq0\) as concentration. sim_bayes_vm <- function(n, n_sim = 100, mu_true = 2, kappa_true = 4, prior = list(c = 0, R0 = 0, mu0 = 0), n_iter = 10000, burn = 5000, seed = 2024) { c <- prior$c R0 <- prior$R0 mu0 <- prior$mu0 cover_mu <- numeric(n_sim) cover_kappa <- numeric(n_sim) len_mu <- numeric(n_sim) len_kappa <- numeric(n_sim) mean_mu <- numeric(n_sim) mean_kappa <- numeric(n_sim) runtimes <- numeric(n_sim) set.seed(seed) for (i in 1:n_sim) { theta <- as.numeric(rvonmises(n, circular(mu_true), kappa_true)) C_n <- R0*cos(mu0) + sum(cos(theta)) S_n <- R0*sin(mu0) + sum(sin(theta)) Rn <- sqrt(C_n^2 + S_n^2) theta_bar_n <- atan2(S_n, C_n) eta <- c + n Rbar <- Rn / n if (Rbar < 0.001) { kappa_init <- 0 } else if (Rbar > 0.999) { kappa_init <- 1 / (2 * (1 - Rbar)) } else { solve_kappa <- function(k) besselI(k,1,expon.scaled=TRUE)/besselI(k,0,expon.scaled=TRUE)-Rbar kappa_init <- uniroot(solve_kappa, c(0.001, 100))$root } mu_init <- theta_bar_n mu_sam <- numeric(n_iter) kappa_sam <- numeric(n_iter) mu_sam[1] <- mu_init kappa_sam[1] <- kappa_init rvm_one <- function(mean_rad, kappa) { as.numeric(rvonmises(1, circular(mean_rad), kappa)) } t0 <- proc.time()[3] for (iter in 2:n_iter) { mu_sam[iter] <- rvm_one(theta_bar_n, kappa_sam[iter-1] * Rn) etag <- -Rn * cos(mu_sam[iter] - theta_bar_n) kappa_sam[iter] <- circglmbayes:::sampleKappa(etag = etag, eta = eta)[1] } t1 <- proc.time()[3] runtimes[i] <- t1 - t0 mu_post <- mu_sam[-(1:burn)] kappa_post <- kappa_sam[-(1:burn)] ci_mu <- quantile(mu_post, c(0.025, 0.975)) ci_kappa <- quantile(kappa_post, c(0.025, 0.975)) cover_mu[i] <- (mu_true >= ci_mu[1] & mu_true <= ci_mu[2]) cover_kappa[i] <- (kappa_true >= ci_kappa[1] & kappa_true <= ci_kappa[2]) len_mu[i] <- ci_mu[2] - ci_mu[1] len_kappa[i] <- ci_kappa[2] - ci_kappa[1] mean_mu[i] <- mean(mu_post) mean_kappa[i] <- mean(kappa_post) } data.frame( n = n, Parameter = c("mu", "kappa"), Coverage = 100 * c(mean(cover_mu), mean(cover_kappa)), Length = c(mean(len_mu), mean(len_kappa)), Max_SE = c(sd(mean_mu), sd(mean_kappa)), Runtime = mean(runtimes) ) } res_bayes10 <- sim_bayes_vm(n = 10, n_sim = 100) res_bayes30 <- sim_bayes_vm(n = 30, n_sim = 100) res_bayes100 <- sim_bayes_vm(n = 100, n_sim = 100) result_table <- rbind(res_bayes10, res_bayes30, res_bayes100) print(result_table, digits = 3) 6.3 Predictive Posterior via Martingale. Score based martingale sampling without pre-conditioner (inverse info). For efficiency, hybrid trick is adopted. # ---- Simulation study wrapper ---- #' Run a simulation study for the martingale posterior (no preconditioner). #' #' @param n number of observed data points per simulated dataset #' @param n_sim number of simulated datasets #' @param mu_true true mean direction (radians), default = 2 #' @param kappa_true true concentration parameter, default = 4 #' @param B number of chains (posterior samples) per dataset #' @param nmc number of predictive resampling steps (hybrid = FALSE) #' @param hybrid logical; if TRUE use hybrid acceleration #' @param M truncation point for hybrid acceleration #' @param post_correct logical; if TRUE apply post‑hoc variance correction #' @param save_chains logical; if TRUE, return all chain details (large memory) #' @param seed random seed #' @param n_cores number of CPU cores for parallelisation (1 = serial) #' #' @return If save_chains = FALSE, a data.frame with coverage rates, #' interval lengths, and mean runtime. If save_chains = TRUE, a list #' containing `summary` and the raw chain objects. sim_martingale_study <- function(n, n_sim = 100, mu_true = 2, kappa_true = 4, B = 200, nmc = 500, hybrid = FALSE, M = 20, post_correct = FALSE, save_chains = FALSE, seed = 1234, n_cores = 1) { # Load required packages inside the function to avoid side effects when used in a package requireNamespace("rstiefel", quietly = TRUE) requireNamespace("circular", quietly = TRUE) # ---- Storage ---- cover_mu <- cover_kappa <- numeric(n_sim) len_mu <- len_kappa <- numeric(n_sim) mean_mu_vec <- mean_kappa_vec <- numeric(n_sim) times <- numeric(n_sim) set.seed(seed) # ---- Parallelisation setup (Windows and Unix) ---- if (n_cores > 1) { if (.Platform$OS.type == "windows") { cl <- parallel::makeCluster(n_cores) parallel::clusterExport(cl, c("get_Ap", "score_fn", "rvMF2", "compute_weight_sum", "run_single_martingale")) on.exit(parallel::stopCluster(cl)) par_fun <- function(...) parallel::parLapply(cl, ...) } else { par_fun <- function(...) parallel::mclapply(..., mc.cores = n_cores) } } else { par_fun <- lapply } # ---- Simulation loop ---- for (i in 1:n_sim) { # Generate observations theta_obs <- as.numeric(circular::rvonmises(n, circular::circular(mu_true), kappa_true)) # Compute MLE as initial natural parameter c0 Cobs <- sum(cos(theta_obs)); Sobs <- sum(sin(theta_obs)) Robs <- sqrt(Cobs^2 + Sobs^2) Rbar <- Robs / n mu_hat <- atan2(Sobs, Cobs) if (Rbar < 0.001) { kappa_hat <- 0 } else if (Rbar > 0.999) { kappa_hat <- 1 / (2 * (1 - Rbar)) } else { kappa_hat <- uniroot(function(k) get_Ap(k, 2) - Rbar, c(0.001, 100))$root } c0 <- kappa_hat * c(cos(mu_hat), sin(mu_hat)) # Run B independent chains t0 <- proc.time()[3] chains <- par_fun(1:B, function(b) run_single_martingale(c0 = c0, N = n, nmc = nmc, hybrid = hybrid, M = M, post_correct = post_correct, p = 2)) t1 <- proc.time()[3] times[i] <- t1 - t0 # Extract samples mu_sam <- sapply(chains, `[[`, "mu_final") kappa_sam <- sapply(chains, `[[`, "kappa_final") mean_mu_vec[i] <- mean(mu_sam) mean_kappa_vec[i] <- mean(kappa_sam) # 95% credible intervals for mu and kappa ci_mu <- quantile(mu_sam, c(0.025, 0.975)) cover_mu[i] <- (mu_true >= ci_mu[1]) & (mu_true <= ci_mu[2]) len_mu[i] <- ci_mu[2] - ci_mu[1] ci_kappa <- quantile(kappa_sam, c(0.025, 0.975)) cover_kappa[i] <- (kappa_true >= ci_kappa[1]) & (kappa_true <= ci_kappa[2]) len_kappa[i] <- ci_kappa[2] - ci_kappa[1] } # ---- Build output table ---- result <- data.frame( n = n, Coverage_mu = mean(cover_mu) * 100, Length_mu = mean(len_mu), Coverage_kappa = mean(cover_kappa) * 100, Length_kappa = mean(len_kappa), Max_SE_mu = sd(mean_mu_vec), Max_SE_kappa = sd(mean_kappa_vec), Mean_Time = mean(times) ) if (save_chains) { list(summary = result, chains = chains) } else { result } } Note here for simplicity, I only test on with 500 chains res_mart10 <- sim_martingale_study(n=10,n_sim = 1, B = 500, nmc =50, hybrid = TRUE, M = 50, post_correct = TRUE,n_cores = 6, save_chains = TRUE) res_mart30 <- sim_martingale_study(n=30,n_sim = 50, B = 500, nmc =50, hybrid = TRUE, M = 50, post_correct = TRUE,n_cores = 6) res_mart100 <- sim_martingale_study(n=100,n_sim = 50, B = 500, nmc =50, hybrid = TRUE, M = 50, post_correct = TRUE,n_cores = 6) result_table <- rbind(res_mart10, res_mart30, res_mart100)
